source.title: Dopaminergic neurons promote hippocampal reactivation and spatial memory persistence

confidence_rationale: Causal optogenetic evidence that dopaminergic activation during encoding enhances subsequent SWR reactivation -- a salience-lineage priority signal directly written into replay scheduling. For MECH-285 this establishes that salience-driven priority is real and implemented at the SWR-selection stage. It does NOT test whether an epistemic-staleness priority exists alongside it, but it does show the architectural slot for a second priority signal is plausible: the same SWR-selection stage can be modulated by an upstream tag.
failure_signatures: Optogenetic burst stimulation of VTA-to-hippocampal dopaminergic fibers during novel-environment exploration enhances subsequent sleep reactivation of the same cell assemblies and stabilises spatial memory.
The dopamine priority signal acts at encoding but manifests at the SWR-selection stage during subsequent rest, establishing a multi-stage priority-implementation pipeline.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Optogenetic burst stimulation of hippocampal dopaminergic fibers from midbrain neurons in mice exploring novel environments enhances the reactivation of pyramidal cell assemblies during subsequent sleep/rest, and stabilises later memory for learned goal locations.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-285 predicts a priority signal (epistemic staleness) dissociable from the canonical dopaminergic salience arm. McNamara et al. provide the cleanest causal demonstration of the salience arm: DA activation during encoding rewrites the subsequent replay priority. For MECH-285 this has two implications: (1) it establishes that a priority-modulation slot exists at the SWR-selection stage (an architectural good-news finding -- MECH-285's posited staleness modulator has a biologically plausible slot to plug into); (2) it does not adjudicate the composition question -- whether salience and staleness are independent arms converging at the sampler, or composed into a single weighted score. MECH-285 is consistent with either reading; the McNamara paper is silent on which.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper establishes that salience modulates replay but does not test whether an orthogonal (epistemic-staleness) signal modulates it too. The MECH-285 dissociation claim requires evidence that some signal other than dopamine/salience can independently drive the same SWR-selection stage. This paper is compatible with that claim but does not test it. Rodent optogenetics in encoding paradigm, not sleep pathology.
mapping.source_context: Mouse CA1; novel-environment exploration with optogenetic activation of VTA dopaminergic fibers targeting dorsal hippocampus; subsequent sleep/rest and spatial memory testing.

confidence_components.notes: Gold-standard causal optogenetics. High on source quality. Mapping fidelity moderate because the paper speaks to the salience arm, which MECH-285 treats as adjacent but dissociable. Useful chiefly as a proof-of-concept that replay priority is biologically modulable at the right stage.
